title: Distance: How to Measure Nearness
concept: How distance defines nearness, using a simple map grid (Manhattan vs. straight-line distance conceptually, no formulas).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the core idea behind the k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) algorithm?

options.text: Classifying a new point by looking at the category of its k closest neighbors and letting them vote.
Grouping all data points into a single large cluster.
Using a series of rules like a decision tree.

exercises.explain: Great job! kNN classifies new data points based on a majority vote of their closest neighbors.

cards.title: Defining 'near'
cards.body: If we say a coffee shop is 'near' you, what do we mean? Is it the straight-line distance a bird would fly, or the actual blocks you have to walk along the streets? In machine learning, we must define 'near' using numbers so our model knows which neighbors are closest.

cards.title: Distance metrics
cards.body: A distance metric is a rule that calculates the distance between two points. kNN uses these calculations to find which neighbors are closest on our data map.

cards.title: Two ways to measure
cards.body: Imagine a city grid. If you walk along the streets, you go up and across — this is called Manhattan distance (or grid distance). If you fly straight over the buildings, it's a direct line — this is called straight-line distance (or Euclidean distance).

cards.title: On the grid
cards.body: To go from point A (0,0) to point B (3,4) on a grid: Manhattan distance is 3 blocks east + 4 blocks north = 7 blocks. Straight-line distance is the diagonal shortcut, which is exactly 5 blocks. Different distance metrics change who your closest neighbors are!

cards.title: Not just physical space
cards.body: You might think distance only applies to physical maps. But in AI, "space" can represent anything! If we map customers by Age on the horizontal axis and Spending on the vertical axis, two people with similar age and spending are "close" to each other.

cards.title: Visualizing distance
cards.body: See how the two distance measurements differ on a grid:

left.text:
The shortest diagonal path
directly between two points.
Like a bird flying.

right.text:
Walking along grid lines only.
Calculated by adding horizontal
and vertical steps.

exercises.prompt: How does Manhattan distance measure the distance between two points on a grid?

options.text: By adding the horizontal and vertical steps along the grid lines.
By drawing a straight diagonal line directly between them.
By measuring the curved path of a circle around them.

exercises.explain: Correct! Manhattan distance is like walking city blocks — you can only move along the grid lines, not diagonally.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Changing the distance metric from straight-line to Manhattan distance can change which neighbors are considered 'closest'.
exercises.explain: True! Because they measure paths differently, a neighbor that is closest in a straight line might not be the closest when walking along grid blocks.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence comparing the two distance types:
exercises.sentence: Flying directly from point A to point B represents ___ distance, while walking along grid streets represents Manhattan distance.
exercises.explain: Straight-line distance (or Euclidean distance) measures the direct diagonal shortcut between two points.

exercises.prompt: If we map customers based on Age and Income, which pair of customers is 'closest'?

options.text: Customer A (Age 25, Income $40k) and Customer B (Age 26, Income $41k)
Customer A (Age 25, Income $40k) and Customer C (Age 60, Income $100k)
Customer A (Age 25, Income $40k) and Customer D (Age 25, Income $10k)

exercises.explain: Yes! Customer A and B are very close in both Age (difference of 1) and Income (difference of 1k), meaning they have a tiny distance between them.

exercises.prompt: You want to find the nearest pizza shop on a grid-based city map. Shop X is 3 blocks East and 2 blocks North. Shop Y is 4 blocks East and 0 blocks North. If you can only walk along grid streets (Manhattan distance), which shop is closer, and what is its distance?

options.text: Shop Y is closer, with a distance of 4 blocks (4 + 0).
Shop X is closer, with a distance of 5 blocks (3 + 2).
Both shops are at the exact same distance of 5 blocks.
Shop X is closer, because diagonal distance is always shorter.

exercises.explain: Brilliant! For Shop X, the Manhattan distance is 3 + 2 = 5 blocks. For Shop Y, the Manhattan distance is 4 + 0 = 4 blocks. Since 4 is less than 5, Shop Y is closer when walking along streets!
